description: This figure presents SEM micrographs paired with EDS line scanning analysis of Fe–10Mn–10Al–0.7C low-density steel specimens annealed at (a) 800 °C and (b) 900 °C for 1 hour. The upper panels show backscattered electron images revealing the microstructural evolution: at 800 °C, distinct κ-carbide particles (indicated by yellow arrows) are visible along α/γ and α/α boundaries within the ferrite (α) and austenite (γ) matrix, while at 900 °C, κ-carbides have largely dissolved leaving a dual-phase α+γ structure. The lower panels display quantitative EDS line scans (Al K, Mn K, Fe K) acquired along the indicated white lines, demonstrating elemental partitioning among phases. At 800 °C, pronounced composition peaks reveal that κ-carbide exhibits the highest Mn and Al contents compared to the surrounding α and γ phases, with Mn enrichment and Al depletion in γ relative to α. At 900 °C, the line profiles show uniform elemental distribution without κ-carbide signature peaks, confirming the dissolution of κ-phase. These results support the conclusion that κ-carbide serves as a strong sink for Mn and Al, that γ-phase is Mn-rich and Al-lean compared to α-phase, and that the dissolution temperature of κ-carbide lies between 850 °C and 900 °C, with higher thermal stability at α/α boundaries than at α/γ boundaries.
